title: Pommes Anna
subtitle: Thinly sliced waxy potatoes layered and baked crisp in butter
excerpt: Waxy potatoes sliced paper-thin, layered in concentric circles with melted butter, and baked in a heavy pan until the outside is deeply golden and crisp and the inside is tender and yielding. Unmoulded to serve, it looks like a disc of burnished bronze. It is one of the great potato dishes.
sourceNotes:
Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). Pommes Anna entry: waxy potato, clarified butter, heavy pan, no seasoning other than salt. The dish named for Anna Deslions, a demi-mondaine of Second Empire Paris, by chef Adolphe Dugléré.

Prosper Montagné, Larousse Gastronomique (1938). Pommes Anna: the importance of a very hot oven and a heavy cast-iron or copper pan; the correct layering and pressing technique.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be baked, cooled, and refrigerated in the pan for up to 24 hours. Reheat in a hot oven (200°C fan) for 15 to 20 minutes until the outside is crisped again.

glossaryTerms.definition: Butter with the milk solids and water removed by melting and skimming, leaving only the pure fat. It has a higher smoke point than whole butter and does not burn at the high temperatures needed for pommes Anna. To make it: melt butter gently, skim the white foam from the surface, then pour off the clear yellow fat, leaving the milky sediment behind.

content.text: Pommes Anna is layers of waxy potato sliced thin, packed tightly into a heavy pan with generous amounts of butter, and baked at high heat until the outside is deeply golden and the layers meld into a tender, cohesive cake. It is unmoulded to serve: a flat disc, uniformly crisp on the outside and yielding within. The only ingredients are potato, butter, and salt.
The technique has two demands: thin, uniform slices (a mandoline makes this reliable; a knife requires patience), and
clarified butter
. Whole butter burns at the temperature needed to crisp the base; clarified butter does not. If you do not want to clarify butter, use ghee instead: it is the same thing, sold ready-made.

Use a waxy variety: Charlotte, Anya, or similar. Floury potatoes collapse rather than staying in clean layers. Avoid rinsing the slices after cutting; the surface starch helps the layers bind together.
What you need
You need a 22 to 24 cm cast-iron skillet or heavy ovenproof frying pan, and a mandoline for even slices. The pan goes from hob to oven, so it must have no plastic handles.
Method
Clarify the butter

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter in a small pan over low heat. White foam will rise to the surface. Skim it off with a spoon. Let the butter settle for a minute, then pour the clear yellow fat carefully through a sieve into a jug, leaving the milky sediment behind. This is your
clarifying butter
. Set aside.
Slice the potatoes
Slice the {{potato}} potatoes on a mandoline to about 3 mm thick. Keep the slices as they come; do not rinse or soak them. Lay them out and season lightly with salt.
Build the layers

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Brush the cast-iron skillet generously with clarified butter and heat it on the hob over medium-high heat. When the butter is shimmering, begin building the layers. Start with the neatest, most evenly-sized slices arranged in concentric overlapping circles on the base of the pan: this first layer will become the visible top of the finished dish. Brush with clarified butter and season lightly. Continue building layers, pressing each one down firmly, brushing with butter, and seasoning lightly as you go. Each layer need not be as neat as the first; you are building a compact cake.
Crisp the base and bake

Cook the pan on the hob over medium-high heat for 5 to 6 minutes until you can hear the base sizzling and the edges are beginning to colour. Press the top of the potato cake firmly with a spatula. Transfer to the oven and bake for 40 to 45 minutes. After 25 minutes, press the top down firmly again with the back of a spoon. The potatoes are done when the top is golden and a sharp knife slides through the layers without resistance.
Rest and unmould

Remove from the oven and leave to rest in the pan for 5 minutes. Run a palette knife around the edge. Set a plate or board larger than the pan firmly on top and invert in one decisive movement. The potato cake should land with its crisp base facing up. If any slices stick to the pan, lift them back into place. Serve immediately or return to the oven briefly to re-crisp if needed.

items.symptom: Base burns before the potato is cooked through
items.cause: Hob heat too high, or base layer not pressed down firmly enough
items.fix: Press the layers down hard as you build to close air gaps. Use medium heat for the pre-oven crisping stage. Check the base colour before moving to the oven

items.cause: Floury potato used, or slices rinsed and lost their starch
items.fix: Use a waxy variety and do not rinse the slices; the surface starch acts as the glue between layers

items.cause: Too much butter, or whole butter used at too high a temperature
items.fix: Use clarified butter (or ghee); apply it in thin coats between layers rather than pouring generously

items.symptom: Edges cook faster than the centre
items.cause: Pan too small for the amount of potato, or oven not fully up to temperature
items.fix: Make sure the oven is fully preheated; use a pan that allows the layers to come no higher than 3 to 4 cm before baking

content.text: Where this dish lives

Pommes Anna is said to have been created in the 1860s by Adolphe Dugléré, chef at the Café Anglais in Paris. The dish entered the classical repertoire quickly, appearing in Escoffier's catalogue at the turn of the century as a test of technique and judgment. It asks nothing from the cook except attention and good butter. Served alongside a roast or a braise, or as the main event of a simple dinner, it is one of those preparations that improves every meal it accompanies.
